title: Shinjis Revelation AKA Bloodied Hands Asuka vs Rei
display_title: Shinjis Revelation AKA Bloodied Hands Asuka vs Rei
wikitext:
[]: Shinji's Revelation (AKA: Bloodied Hands) - Asuka vs Rei             Quote Post by Sachi » Sun Apr 17, 2011 12:08 am In my need to lay the foundation of my post, let me give you a little summary of the relevant events. As we know, Asuka chooses to pilot Unit-03, but is hijacked by the 9th Angel/Bardiel, forcing a showdown between Unit-03 and Unit-01. Upon refusing to fight, Shinji's control of his Evangelion is relinquished by Gendo and the Dummy System brutally destroys Unit-03. This mirrors the original series, with the only major difference of Asuka piloting instead of Toji. Just as in the original series, Shinji blames his father for using his hands to destroy Unit-03 and his friend (Toji or Asuka, depending on the continuity). Essentially, Shinji feels that his hands of been bloodied by force of his father, and this ultimately leads to him quitting Nerv and running away once more. But wait, bloodied hands? Where else do we see this? Oh, yes! Just after Shinji witnesses the 10th Angel/Zeruel devour Unit-00 and Rei! Note that this blood came directly from the bleeding Unit-02 while Shinji was in the emergency shelter. As we should be familiar with, and despite in this one instance that Unit-02 was piloted by Mari, Unit-02 is an avatar for Asuka Langely Shikinami. Figuratively speaking, this blood is Asuka's. In this shot we see that Shinji is reflecting upon his hands already bearing the blood of Asuka at this point, just as he witnesses Rei being eaten. By remembering what he did to Asuka, Shinji realizes that the blood of Rei may also be on his hands because of his failure to assist her in battle. He sees that the blood on his hands currently may soon belong to Rei. However, he is content on not letting this happen; he's content on not bearing the guilt of another life upon his conscious; he does not want more blood on his hands. So, what does he do? What he should have done all along: act! He takes the aggressive stance and pushes forward to do what he finally wants to do. He no longer pilots the Eva for the gratitude of his father; he no longer pilots the Eva because he's told to; he pilots the Eva because it's what he wants to do. And he's even encouraged to do so by Misato just prior to the Third Impact scene. ("Pilot for yourself! Do it because it's what you want to do!"*) Many have interpreted his actions for Rei as selfish, and indicative that he does not give a rat's ass about Asuka (because if he did he OBVIOUSLY would have bitched more about it). However, I disagree (well, about the second part). I interpret this scene as Shinji learning from his mistakes, and fighting to not make the same mistake again. He realizes that he fucked up in saving Asuka, and vows not to fuck up again, thus his powerful attempt to save Rei and his disregard of what happens to himself or anybody else. Is this selfish? Okay, yeah, sort of. He doesn't realize what he's getting himself into. But he finally found what drives him, and that's really all the matters for this arc in his development. And clearly, a lot of this was manipulated by Gendo and Fuyutsuki in order to awaken Unit-01, so in a way Shinji is still being controlled (there's that ever-obsessive need for control we all love in Gendo!). However, one of the primary inner-conflicts Shinji bears is his questioning of why he pilots the Eva, and this conflict is now resolved in him thanks to this revelation he has. So, thoughts? Feelings? Discuss! * I'm paraphrasing here.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Apr 17, 2011 1:22 am The hand motif stuff hasn't been explored too much, but most of the conclusions have been forwarded in the Shinji threads. For example, I've been saying for a while that Operation Save Ayanami is basically Shinji compensating for his failure in the Eva-03 incident. I still wonder where the hell Shinji gets the idea that he can save Rei after he sees Eva-00 get fucking eaten by an Angel... Wouldn't it be logical to assume that, I dunno, she's DEAD? I guess he must have read the script.

[]:             Quote Post by 1731298478 » Sun Apr 17, 2011 1:39 am That's quite possible. I think that particular shot also evokes this one. As far as Gendo manipulating Shinji goes, there seems to be a parallel, in that - especially given how the 1.11 sequence is composed compared with the original TV version - it is almost as though Gendo is counting on Shinji's reaction, or is using Rei in order to induce Shinji to pilot. In that sense, the later scene, where Gendo's intention is fulfilled, is partially modeled upon the earlier moment. I think, as well, with Shinji, that his dialogue in episode 25' (talking with Misato) strongly implies that the main reason he will no longer pilot Eva is due to his having killed Kaworu (compare the beginning of episode 25). To kill human beings - especially, of course, those close to him - is truly hateful for Shinji. In the case of Toji or Asuka, he could blame his father, and may even have wished to kill his father, regardless of what the consequences of that act might have been. In the case of Kaworu, he could only wish to destroy himself. In any case, if the implication of the final scene is that Shinji would have been destroying Rei II along with Zeruel's core, a version of the dilemma over Kaworu reasserts itself. The suggestion in the Tsurumaki interview that Kaworu arrived to find that Rei had in some sense usurped his place might relate to that.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Sun Apr 17, 2011 8:43 am 1731298478 wrote: In any case, if the implication of the final scene is that Shinji would have been destroying Rei II along with Zeruel's core, a version of the dilemma over Kaworu reasserts itself. The suggestion in the Tsurumaki interview that Kaworu arrived to find that Rei had in some sense usurped his place might relate to that.

[]:             Quote Post by SaltyJoe » Sun Apr 17, 2011 8:57 am I would like to add that he also has dirt on his hands alongside Unit-02's blood, just like when Kaji lectured him on survivor responsibility. Yeah, the intended meaning is to depict Shinji as being spurred by his inabilitiy to act earlier to take action to prevent someone's death. Whether this newfound sense of purpose will last in the face of setbacks that will surely come is another question. 1731298478 wrote: The suggestion in the Tsurumaki interview that Kaworu arrived to find that Rei had in some sense usurped his place might relate to that.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sun Apr 17, 2011 9:20 am Reichu wrote: I still wonder where the hell Shinji gets the idea that he can save Rei after he sees Eva-00 get rogering eaten by an Angel... Wouldn't it be logical to assume that, I dunno, she's DEAD? I guess he must have read the script.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Sun Apr 17, 2011 9:33 am NemZ wrote: That's quite possibly a bigger plot hole than 24, really.

[]:             Quote Post by SaltyJoe » Sun Apr 17, 2011 9:41 am Also, Mari quips Shinji on about "the Angel trying to merge with the pilot", implying that there is still a Rei to merge with. And Mari knows because she is hax like that.

[]:             Quote Post by Knuckles » Sun Apr 17, 2011 10:33 am Above post is right. You could say it was a mix between 'Asuka's' blood and Mari's reverse psychology that spurred Shinji into his rage to save Rei. It wasn't a plothole at all.

[]:             Quote Post by Kodaemon » Sun Apr 17, 2011 11:54 am Unit 00 was swallowed whole, so Shinji might have initially reasoned he still had the chance to rip the angel to pieces and just physically get the entry plug out. And once he goes into god mode, reason goes out the window anyway.

[]:             Quote Post by fadadio » Sun Apr 17, 2011 1:43 pm Sachi wrote: A drive of passion doesn't really need logic attached to it.

[]:             Quote Post by MaskedOmega » Sun Apr 17, 2011 4:54 pm I really like this theory but what I do not get is how Shinji thought he could bring Rei back and it caused him to go angelic, it seemed kind of random. It would have made more sense for him to go angelic first and when he is about to finish the angel off, he sees Rei in the S2 organ or something similar and then attempts to rescue her.

[]:             Quote Post by Temjin » Sun Apr 17, 2011 4:59 pm MaskedOmega wrote: It would have made more sense for him to go angelic first and when he is about to finish the angel off, he sees Rei in the S2 organ or something similar and then attempts to rescue her.

[]:             Quote Post by Trajan » Sun Apr 17, 2011 7:19 pm Remember, Unit 01 turns into a giant wish granting machine so anything was technically possible. Maybe Shinji was aware of this subconsciously or through his connection with the soul of Unit 0? Besides, maybe being absorbed by an angel is just like Unit 01 absorbing Shinji and Yui. You don't technically die either, you just become...disembodied. How Shinji would know this? Because Rebuild is a squeal to the original series. Well, that's why we fanwank.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Sun Apr 17, 2011 9:40 pm Trajan wrote: Remember, Unit 01 turns into a giant wish granting machine so anything was technically possible. Maybe Shinji was aware of this subconsciously or through his connection with the soul of Unit 0?

[]:             Quote Post by 1731298478 » Mon Apr 18, 2011 6:29 am Sachi wrote: I don't believe the implication is that Shinji would be destroying Rei. Shinji honestly believes that he has the power to save her as she is. [...] There's no moral dilemma for Shinji in this scene; he's made up his mind and is doing everything in his power to act upon his decision.

[]:             Quote Post by fadadio » Mon Apr 18, 2011 11:38 am 1731298478 wrote: Right. I do think that this is very plausibly the case. At the same time, there is a section where, despite Shinji having made up his mind to fight Zeruel, the audience can have no idea that he is intending to save Rei or that saving Rei is even possible. That is, during a first viewing, with no foreknowledge. Let me try to elaborate by returning for a second to the thread's initial idea: Shinji refused to pilot Eva because he ended up almost killing Asuka without being able to come to her aid; piloting Eva means that he will have to injure or kill human beings, including human beings who are close to him or whom he loves. The sight of the blood on his hands then makes him realize that, in the new situation, not piloting Eva would lead to Rei's death. Therefore, he reverses his decision. However, there is a difficulty which has been pointed out in this thread. How does Shinji infer from the situation that he will be able to save Rei? Mari's words to Shinji as he looks at his hand are: パイロットごと吸収してしまったんだ。君も死んじゃうよ。早く逃げなよ……, "It absorbed the pilot as well. You'll die, too. You must escape quickly." That is to say, Mari tells Shinji that Rei is dead, or as good as dead. Certainly, when I first saw the film, I had no doubt that Rei was effectively dead, and the possibility of Shinji "saving" her never crossed my mind until he declared he was going to do so. If I look back on my initial reaction, it ran something like this: Shinji knows, seeing the apocalyptic landscape and the giant Rei-angel, as well as Kaji's destroyed garden (the destroyed earth), that the end is really near and that he is unwilling to forsake the world. Although Rei, as Mari said, is effectively dead, Shinji will still have to destroy the "form" of Rei or the remnant of Rei absorbed by the angel. For this he will need to realize what he did not realize during the Unit-03 incident. That lesson was actually summed up by Mari in one of the older 2.0 storyboards (from Shinji Higuchi's interview): "Are you stupid? One person almost died? What? Are you a coward? [...] is what Asuka-chan would say if she was here. It's not the case that you could become God because you piloted Eva. There are people you can save, and people you can't." That is to say, if Shinji is to protect the world here, he must recognize that Rei is among the people he can't save, and he must confirm this by destroying the "Rei-angel" voluntarily. That was my initial impression, and I was truly impressed with the way in which the scenario unfolded to reach that point, despite or because of its characteristically "Eva-esque" coldness. In any case, I think the scenario is constructed so as to conceal not only the fact that Shinji will save Rei but, prior to that, to conceal the fact that this is even an option. We do not even know that Shinji is trying to save Rei until his eyes turn red and Unit-01 reactivates. Strictly speaking, it is even possible that Shinji only resolved to try and save Ayanami after his power had expired, and that some change in himself manifests at that moment. However that may be, I think the impact of the scene depends upon this strange and severely disorienting reversal. That's not at all to say that Shinji didn't intend to save Ayanami from the start, or that his motivation wasn't as you conjectured. It's just that this can't be apparent to the audience from the beginning, and it's in this period before we know what Shinji is doing that a different set of questions and feelings arise. Or, to put it another way, the scene is constructed in such a way so as to make the audience aware of, and to reflect upon, alternate courses of action. The problem arises for the audience, even if Shinji himself is unconscious of it. As an aside, I think this dilemma for the audience does not end with Shinji declaring his intention, but continues in a modified form after Shinji speaks to Rei in the core and starts Third Impact, i.e., the start of Third Impact only seems to reinforce the idea that Shinji must renounce or destroy Rei in order to save the world, and the scene seems to build up to Shinji having to accept that difficult fact. It's the disorienting nature of the reversal that leads to the new set of questions: What was Shinji thinking? Was this really the right thing to do? etc., which are especially mysterious owing to the development of the scene. Right, it's certainly a joke, but it's predicated on the real fact that Kaworu was smiling in the original storyboard but they decided in the last minute that he should look upset. So, the question is: what was it that upset Kaworu? Of course, there might be a number of possible answers to this.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Mon Apr 18, 2011 12:28 pm 1731298478 wrote: That lesson was actually summed up by Mari in one of the older 2.0 storyboards (from Shinji Higuchi's interview): "Are you stupid? One person almost died? What? Are you a coward? [...] is what Asuka-chan would say if she was here. It's not the case that you could become God because you piloted Eva. There are people you can save, and people you can't."

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Mon Apr 18, 2011 3:13 pm Reichu wrote: I still wonder where the hell Shinji gets the idea that he can save Rei after he sees Eva-00 get rogering eaten by an Angel... Wouldn't it be logical to assume that, I dunno, she's DEAD? I guess he must have read the script.

[]:             Quote Post by fadadio » Mon Apr 18, 2011 3:44 pm Stryker wrote: I probably should rewatch the movie, but if I witnessed my friend being swallowed whole, I would say, "REEEEEEEVVEEEEEEEEEEEEEEENNGGGGGGGGGEEEEEEEE!!!!!!!*," in which I'd pilot the Evangelion to annihilate the pretentious fiend that ate my "very good best friend". I'm not sure that Shinji had the idea that she was still alive, but I bet, even as much as a coward as he is, would still want revenge. I think the fact that he could get Ayanami back were brownie points for growing a pair.

Strictly speaking, it is even possible that Shinji only resolved to try and save Ayanami after his power had expired, and that some change in himself manifests at that moment. However that may be, I think the impact of the scene depends upon this strange and severely disorienting reversal. That's not at all to say that Shinji didn't intend to save Ayanami from the start, or that his motivation wasn't as you conjectured. It's just that this can't be apparent to the audience from the beginning, and it's in this period before we know what Shinji is doing that a different set of questions and feelings arise. Or, to put it another way, the scene is constructed in such a

Article: Shinjis Revelation AKA Bloodied Hands Asuka vs Rei
 can't be apparent to the audience from the beginning, and it's in this period before we know what Shinji is doing that a different set of questions and feelings arise. Or, to put it another way, the scene is constructed in such a way so as to make the audience aware of, and to reflect upon, alternate courses of action. The problem arises for the audience, even if Shinji himself is unconscious of it. As an aside, I think this dilemma for the audience does not end with Shinji declaring his intention, but continues in a modified form after Shinji speaks to Rei in the core and starts Third Impact, i.e., the start of Third Impact only seems to reinforce the idea that Shinji must renounce or destroy Rei in order to save the world, and the scene seems to build up to Shinji having to accept that difficult fact. It's the disorienting nature of the reversal that leads to the new set of questions: What was Shinji thinking? Was this really the right thing to do? etc., which are especially mysterious owing to the development of the scene. Right, it's certainly a joke, but it's predicated on the real fact that Kaworu was smiling in the original storyboard but they decided in the last minute that he should look upset. So, the question is: what was it that upset Kaworu? Of course, there might be a number of possible answers to this.
